class_schedule.topic: Agile Principles and the Agile Manifesto
learning_objectives: Articulate the four values and twelve principles of the Agile Manifesto
Compare predictive, adaptive, and hybrid project approaches
Identify when agile methods are most appropriate
readings: Beck et al., Manifesto for Agile Software Development

lecture_notes.introduction: Agile is not a methodology but a mindset. It values responding to change over following a plan, and it works best when requirements are uncertain and feedback is frequent.
key_concepts: Four values: individuals/interactions over processes/tools, working product over documentation, customer collaboration over contract negotiation, responding to change over following a plan
Twelve principles: satisfy the customer, welcome change, deliver frequently, business and developers together, motivated individuals
Predictive (waterfall) vs. adaptive (agile) vs. hybrid approaches
Stacey complexity model: simple, complicated, complex, chaotic domains
When agile fits: uncertain requirements, frequent feedback possible, small cross-functional teams
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing a waterfall project plan with an agile product backlog for the same product

review.value: Values items on the left MORE, not instead of items on the right; both sides have value
Agile works best in the complex domain where requirements and technology are not fully understood upfront

learning_objectives: Describe the three Scrum roles, five events, and three artifacts
Explain how Scrum implements empirical process control

lecture_notes.introduction: Scrum is the most widely used agile framework. It implements empirical process control through transparency, inspection, and adaptation.
key_concepts: Roles: Product Owner (what to build), Scrum Master (process facilitation), Development Team (how to build)
Events: Sprint, Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective
Artifacts: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment (with Definition of Done)
Sprint: time-boxed iteration (1-4 weeks) producing a potentially releasable increment
Empiricism: transparency (everyone sees the work), inspection (regular check-ins), adaptation (adjust based on findings)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a simulated sprint planning session with a sample product backlog
setup_requirements: Sample product backlog with user stories

review.value: Fixed time-box; scope can be negotiated but the deadline does not move; delivers a done increment
Shared understanding of what complete means; all work must meet DoD before it counts as done

class_schedule.topic: User Stories, Estimation, and Backlog Management
learning_objectives: Write user stories with acceptance criteria using the INVEST model
Estimate work using story points and planning poker
Prioritize the product backlog using MoSCoW and value vs. effort
readings: Cohn, Agile Estimating and Planning, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: User stories capture requirements from the user's perspective. They are the building blocks of the product backlog.
key_concepts: User story format: As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]
INVEST criteria: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable
Acceptance criteria: given-when-then format for verifiable conditions
Story points: relative sizing using Fibonacci sequence (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21)
Planning poker: team consensus estimation technique
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing user stories for a food delivery app and estimating them with planning poker

review.value: Measure relative complexity/effort, not hours; a 5-point story is roughly 2.5 times the effort of a 2-point story
Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have (this time); provides clear priority tiers

learning_objectives: Implement a Kanban board with WIP limits
Calculate and optimize cycle time and throughput
Compare Scrum and Kanban for different project contexts
readings: Anderson, Kanban: Successful Evolutionary Change, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Kanban is a flow-based agile method that visualizes work, limits work in progress, and optimizes throughput.
key_concepts: Kanban board: visualize workflow with columns for each stage
WIP limits: restrict the number of items in each stage to prevent bottlenecks
Cycle time: time from work started to work done; key metric for predictability
Throughput: number of items completed per time period
Cumulative flow diagram: shows WIP, throughput, and cycle time trends
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a Kanban board for a marketing campaign project and simulating flow with WIP limits
setup_requirements: Sticky notes or Kanban tool (Trello, Jira)

review.value: Stop starting, start finishing; limiting WIP reduces context switching and reveals bottlenecks
Scrum uses time-boxed iterations with roles; Kanban uses continuous flow with WIP limits; both can coexist

class_schedule.topic: Agile Metrics, Velocity, and Release Planning
learning_objectives: Create a release plan with feature roadmap
Use burndown and burnup charts for sprint and release tracking
readings: Cohn, Agile Estimating and Planning, Ch. 14-18

lecture_notes.introduction: Agile teams use empirical data, not wishful thinking, to plan releases and track progress.
key_concepts: Velocity: story points completed per sprint; stabilizes after 3-4 sprints
Release planning: total story points / velocity = number of sprints needed
Sprint burndown: tracks remaining work within a sprint, updated daily
Release burnup: tracks cumulative work completed against total scope
Escaped defects, team happiness, and customer satisfaction as additional metrics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a release plan for a 60-story-point product backlog using historical velocity data

review.value: Descriptive, not prescriptive; use it for forecasting, never as a performance target that pressures the team
Shows both work done and total scope; reveals scope changes that burndown charts hide

learning_objectives: Compare scaling frameworks: SAFe, LeSS, and Nexus
Design a hybrid approach combining agile and predictive elements
Navigate organizational resistance to agile adoption

lecture_notes.introduction: Scaling agile beyond a single team introduces coordination challenges. Hybrid approaches let organizations adopt agile incrementally.
key_concepts: SAFe: Scaled Agile Framework with portfolio, program, and team levels
LeSS: Large-Scale Scrum keeps Scrum simple with multiple teams on one product
Nexus: extends Scrum with an integration team for 3-9 Scrum teams
Hybrid approaches: agile execution within a predictive governance framework
Organizational change management for agile transformations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a hybrid approach for a regulated industry project with compliance requirements

review.value: Most comprehensive and prescriptive scaling framework; good for large enterprises but can feel heavy
Use predictive for well-understood components and agile for uncertain ones; tailor to the project context

quizzes.topic: Agile Principles, Scrum, and User Stories

questions.question: A team completed 25, 30, 28, and 27 story points in their last four sprints. The remaining product backlog is 110 story points. Estimate how many sprints are needed to complete the backlog and explain your reasoning.
questions.answer: Average velocity = (25 + 30 + 28 + 27) / 4 = 110 / 4 = 27.5 story points per sprint. Sprints needed = 110 / 27.5 = 4 sprints. However, velocity varies (range 25-30), so a range estimate is more honest: optimistic = 110/30 = 3.67 (4 sprints), pessimistic = 110/25 = 4.4 (5 sprints). Plan for 4-5 sprints.

rubric.key: Provides range estimate acknowledging velocity variation

questions.question: Write a user story with three acceptance criteria for a library management system's book checkout feature. Apply the INVEST criteria.
questions.answer: User story: As a library member, I want to check out a book using my membership card, so that I can take the book home for the loan period. Acceptance criteria: (1) Given the member has a valid membership and fewer than 5 books checked out, when they scan their card and the book, then the system records the checkout with a due date 21 days from today. (2) Given the member has 5 books already checked out, when they attempt to check out another, then the system displays an error and does not allow the checkout. (3) Given the book is on hold for another member, when any member attempts to check it out, then the system prevents the checkout and directs the book to the hold queue. INVEST: Independent (no dependency on other stories), Negotiable (implementation details flexible), Valuable (directly serves the user), Estimable (well-defined scope), Small (single feature), Testable (acceptance criteria are verifiable).

rubric.key: Correct user story format with role, feature, benefit
Three clear acceptance criteria in given-when-then format

questions.question: A Kanban board has columns: To Do, In Progress (WIP limit 3), Review (WIP limit 2), Done. Currently In Progress has 3 items and Review has 2 items. A developer finishes an In Progress item. What should happen next and why?
questions.answer: The developer should NOT pull a new item from To Do into In Progress. Instead, they should help clear the Review bottleneck by assisting with one of the 2 items in Review. Review is at its WIP limit (2/2), and moving the finished item there would violate the limit (3/2). Only after a Review item moves to Done, making room in Review, can the finished item move forward. The WIP limits exist to prevent bottlenecks and encourage the team to help where work is blocked rather than starting new work.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Review WIP limit would be violated

questions.question: Explain two key differences between a sprint burndown chart and a release burnup chart, and when each is most useful.
questions.answer: Sprint burndown shows remaining work (decreasing line) within a single sprint, updated daily. It is most useful during the sprint for the team to see if they will complete their sprint commitment. Release burnup shows cumulative work completed (increasing line) plus a total scope line across multiple sprints. It is most useful for stakeholders and release planning because it reveals both progress AND scope changes. If scope increases, the burnup chart shows the gap widening, while a burndown chart would just show the line going back up, which is harder to interpret.

rubric.key: Correctly describes burndown (remaining work, single sprint)
Correctly describes burnup (cumulative, multi-sprint, scope visibility)
Explains when each is most useful

major_assignments.title: Agile Project Simulation
major_assignments.description: Run a 3-sprint simulation of a Scrum project. Create the product backlog, conduct sprint planning, execute sprints (with simulated work), run retrospectives, and deliver a release plan based on actual velocity.
objectives: Create a product backlog with at least 30 user stories
Run 3 sprint planning sessions and estimate with story points
Track velocity and produce burndown/burnup charts
Conduct retrospectives and implement improvements across sprints
deliverables: Product backlog with 30+ user stories, acceptance criteria, and story point estimates
Sprint planning records for 3 sprints showing selected stories and capacity
Sprint burndown charts for each sprint
Release burnup chart showing progress and scope changes
Retrospective notes for each sprint with action items
Final release plan based on observed velocity

criteria: 30+ user stories in proper format with acceptance criteria
Stories meet INVEST criteria and are properly sized
Backlog is prioritized using MoSCoW or value/effort
Sprint goals are clear and achievable
Burndown charts show realistic sprint progress
Velocity tracked and used for forecasting
Release burnup chart accurately reflects progress and scope
Release plan is realistic based on observed velocity
Action items from Sprint 1 retro are implemented in Sprint 2
Continuous improvement is demonstrated across sprints
Comprehensive product backlog with quality user stories
Well-executed sprint simulation with realistic data
Effective use of agile metrics for planning
Evidence of continuous improvement through retrospectives

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on agile principles, Scrum, Kanban, and metrics

midterm_examination.description: Agile principles, Scrum, and user story writing

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all agile topics including scaling

class_participation.description: Active participation in sprint simulations, retrospectives, and Kanban exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sprint simulation sessions. Missing a sprint simulation results in zero credit for that sprint.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. Sprint deliverables must be submitted by the sprint deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Sprint simulations may be done in teams of 3-5. The product backlog and release plan are individual deliverables.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All user stories and planning artifacts must be original work. Using story templates from the internet without adaptation is not acceptable.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to a Kanban/Scrum tool (Trello, Jira, or Azure DevOps free tier) and spreadsheet application required.
